metadata.source_file: Long-term economic growth projections in the Shared Socioeconomic Pathways.md
metadata.title: Long-term economic growth projections in the Shared Socioeconomic Pathways
metadata.abstract: Future projections of the impact of international environmental policies, such as those related to climate change, are usually presented against a ‘‘business as usual’’ (BAU) baseline or a reference scenario. For instance, the OECD Environmental Outlook to 2050 (OECD, 2012a) describes in detail a set of socio-economic developments and the related pressures on the environment, and highlights the consequences of policy actions for key environmental themes. Greenhouse gas emissions pathways resulting from economic reference scenarios are then used for positioning mitigation actions, e.g. by defining targets in relation to baseline emission levels. However, as a wide range of possible factors can
metadata.introduction:
Future projections of the impact of international environmental policies, such as those related to climate change, are usually presented against a ‘‘business as usual’’ (BAU) baseline or a reference scenario. For instance, the OECD Environmental Outlook to 2050 (OECD, 2012a) describes in detail a set of socio-economic developments and the related pressures on the environment, and highlights the consequences of policy actions for key environmental themes. Greenhouse gas emissions pathways resulting from economic reference scenarios are then used for positioning mitigation actions, e.g. by defining targets in relation to baseline emission levels. However, as a wide range of possible factors can 

> § This paper does not necessarily represent the views of the OECD or its member countries. The paper has benefited from valuable comments by several colleagues of the OECD Environment Directorate and OECD Economics Department. Special thanks go to Cuauhtemoc Rebolledo (OECD Environment Directorate) for valuable research assistance. Furthermore, we received valuable comments on earlier drafts of the projections and the paper from the other modelling teams involved in quantifying the SSP scenarios. 

- Corresponding author. Tel.: +33 1 4524 1953; fax: +33 1 4524 1953. E-mail address: rob.dellink@oecd.org (R. Dellink). 

affect economic projections, it is useful to consider different possible developments. 

This paper presents and compares a range of reference Gross Domestic Product (GDP) projections based on different perspectives on future socio-economic developments. The scenarios are based on the Shared Socioeconomic Pathways (SSPs). The SSP storylines have been developed by the climate change research community (Van Vuuren et al., 2014; O’Neill et al., 2014). They are part of a framework that combines the socio-economic developments defined by the SSPs with Representative greenhouse gas Concentration Pathways (RCPs) to assess future climatic changes (Moss et al., 2010; Van Vuuren et al., 2012). The economic growth projections developed here therefore represent quantitative data linked to each SSP that can be combined with other information to develop Reference (Baseline) Scenarios for climate impact assessment. These Reference Scenarios assume no climate policies; climate policy scenarios can be added to link the SSPs to the RCPs. 

This paper illustrates a detailed methodology for making consistent long-term economic projections for most countries in the world, using the ENV-Growth model (Chateau et al., 2014a). ENV-Growth projects a gradual process of convergence towards a balanced growth path along the lines of an augmented Solow 

http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2015.06.004 0959-3780/� 2015 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 

R. Dellink et al. / Global Environmental Change 42 (2017) 200–214 

201 

growth model (Barro and Sala-i-Martin, 2004). Projections start in 2018 since the model initially mimics short-term (2012–2017) economic projections of the OECD and the International Monetary Fund (IMF), and then follows a so-called conditional convergence hypothesis: income levels (e.g. GDP per capita) of less developed countries will increase faster than those of more developed economies based on a catch-up effect in the key drivers of per capita economic growth. Specific attention is paid to the development of income generated from the exploitation of the natural resources crude oil and natural gas. 

The methodology is applied to construct pathways of GDP and income (GDP per capita) levels for 184 individual countries, collectively representing 99.9% of global GDP in 2010, plus a stylised ‘‘rest of the world’’ region. Trend projections are made for each of the SSP scenarios by translating SSP storylines into assumptions on the various drivers of growth. This set of scenarios provides a range of future projections of GDP and income for the rest of the 21st century. The SSP scenarios do not cover the full spectrum of plausible economic projections, but they do illustrate a substantial variance in global GDP levels by the end of the century. The methodology can therefore also serve as a basis for different quantitative assessments that involve economic baselines. 

It should be noted that the projections presented in this paper are not predictions of what will happen. They are meant as reference projections for quantitative modelling exercises that rely on long-term global economic baselines. For instance, Integrated Assessment Models (IAMs) need GDP projections in order to derive long-term emission and temperature projections for at least a century ahead. Having a common set of economic growth assumptions across IAMs helps understand the differences in projections of emissions. Similarly, long-term projections for agricultural sectors can benefit from reference projections on economic activity, as these influence various areas e.g. food demand. The projections are subject to large uncertainties, particularly at the country level and for later decades, and should be interpreted with sufficient care. While an internally consistent set of assumptions is used to make these projections, they disregard a wide range of country-specific drivers of economic growth that are outside the narrow economic framework, such as external shocks, governance barriers and feedbacks from environmental damage. Further, these projections are not official OECD projections, but customised projections made by OECD specialists specifically for the SSP scenarios. 

The paper is structured as follows. Section 2 describes the main elements of the SSP scenarios. Section 3 briefly introduces the ENVGrowth model that is used for making the economic projections. Section 4 discusses the data sources for calibrating the model and the interpretation of the different SSP storylines for the drivers of economic growth. Section 5 presents the resulting income projections for the SSP scenarios. An analysis of the implications of these projections for between-country income inequality is presented in Section 6, while Section 7 investigates the influence that forecasts of short-term growth rates play in the long-term projections and Section 8 concludes.

evidence.original_text: This paper presents and compares a range of reference Gross Domestic Product (GDP) projections based on different perspectives on future socio-economic developments. The scenarios are based on the Shared Socioeconomic Pathways (SSPs). The SSP storylines have been developed by the climate change research community (Van Vuuren et al., 2014; O’Neill et al., 2014). They are part of a framework that combines the socio-economic developments defined by the SSPs with Representative greenhouse gas Concentration Pathways (RCPs) to assess future climatic changes (Moss et al., 2010; Van Vuuren et al., 2012). The economic growth projections developed here therefore represent quantitative data linked to each SSP that can be combined with other information to develop Reference (Baseline) Scenarios for climate impact assessment. These Reference Scenarios assume no climate policies; climate policy scenarios can be added to link the SSPs to the RCPs.

This paper presents and compares a range of reference Gross Domestic Product (GDP) projections based on different perspectives on future socio-economic developments. The scenarios are based on the Shared Socioeconomic Pathways (SSPs). The SSP storylines have been developed by the climate change research community (Van Vuuren et al., 2014; O’Neill et al., 2014). They are part of a framework that combines the socio-economic developments defined by the SSPs with Representative greenhouse gas Concentration Pathways (RCPs) to assess future climatic changes (Moss et al., 2010; Van Vuuren et al., 2012). The economic growth projections developed here therefore represent quantitative data linked to each SSP that can be combined with other information to develop Reference (Baseline) Scenarios for climate impact assessment. These Reference Scenarios assume no climate policies; climate policy scenarios can be added to link the SSPs to the RCPs.

This paper illustrates a detailed methodology for making consistent long-term economic projections for most countries in the world, using the ENV-Growth model (Chateau et al., 2014a). ENV-Growth projects a gradual process of convergence towards a balanced growth path along the lines of an augmented Solow growth model (Barro and Sala-i-Martin, 2004). Projections start in 2018 since the model initially mimics short-term (2012–2017) economic projections of the OECD and the International Monetary Fund (IMF), and then follows a so-called conditional convergence hypothesis: income levels (e.g. GDP per capita) of less developed countries will increase faster than those of more developed economies based on a catch-up effect in the key drivers of per capita economic growth. Specific attention is paid to the development of income generated from the exploitation of the natural resources crude oil and natural gas.

This paper illustrates a detailed methodology for making consistent long-term economic projections for most countries in the world, using the ENV-Growth model (Chateau et al., 2014a). ENV-Growth projects a gradual process of convergence towards a balanced growth path along the lines of an augmented Solow growth model (Barro and Sala-i-Martin, 2004). Projections start in 2018 since the model initially mimics short-term (2012–2017) economic projections of the OECD and the International Monetary Fund (IMF), and then follows a so-called conditional convergence hypothesis: income levels (e.g. GDP per capita) of less developed countries will increase faster than those of more developed economies based on a catch-up effect in the key drivers of per capita economic growth. Specific attention is paid to the development of income generated from the exploitation of the natural resources crude oil and natural gas.

The ENV-Growth model is largely based on the "conditional convergence growth" methodology elaborated by the OECD Economics Department by Duval and de la Maisonneuve (2010) and recently updated to project real GDP for OECD countries as well as major emerging economies to 2060 (Johansson et al., 2013). The ENV-Growth model, described in the Annex, applies most elements of this methodology to a longer timeframe, until the end of the century, and to a larger set of countries, including most nonOECD countries. Some differences in the methodologies between ENV-Growth and Johansson et al. (2013) should be underlined. Firstly, the ENV-Growth framework only deals with potential (i.e. full capacity) real GDP projections, and not with short- and medium-term output gaps stemming from excess capacity. Secondly the two exercises of projections adopt different assumptions about convergence of future capital to output ratios, where ENV-Growth assumes a slow convergence of capital to output ratios to a common long-term level. Thirdly, the ENVGrowth model has also been enhanced to include energy both as productive input (Foure´ et al., 2012) and as a generator of resource rents for oil and gas producing countries (World Bank, 2012a). In conclusion, these and other adaptations to the global scope and longer time horizon make the ENV-Growth projections incomparable to those by Johansson et al. (2013).

It should be noted that the projections presented in this paper are not predictions of what will happen. They are meant as reference projections for quantitative modelling exercises that rely on long-term global economic baselines. For instance, Integrated Assessment Models (IAMs) need GDP projections in order to derive long-term emission and temperature projections for at least a century ahead. Having a common set of economic growth assumptions across IAMs helps understand the differences in projections of emissions. Similarly, long-term projections for agricultural sectors can benefit from reference projections on economic activity, as these influence various areas e.g. food demand. The projections are subject to large uncertainties, particularly at the country level and for later decades, and should be interpreted with sufficient care. While an internally consistent set of assumptions is used to make these projections, they disregard a wide range of country-specific drivers of economic growth that are outside the narrow economic framework, such as external shocks, governance barriers and feedbacks from environmental damage. Further, these projections are not official OECD projections, but customised projections made by OECD specialists specifically for the SSP scenarios.

evidence.section: A brief introduction to SSP scenarios
evidence.original_text: This new scenario framework for the integrated analysis of future climate change comprises two main elements (see Moss et al., 2010; Van Vuuren et al., 2012): (i) Representative Concentration Pathways (RCPs) reflecting projections for greenhouse gas concentrations and radiative forcing, and (ii) Shared Socioeconomic Pathways (SSPs) describing different combinations of socio-economic developments and their associated levels of greenhouse gases emissions. The SSPs combine both qualitative and quantitative information on possible future developments of emissions and their main socio-economic drivers, and include projections for population and income. They do not contain estimated impacts of climate policies and can therefore be considered as reference (or baseline) scenarios, reflecting different views on "no climate policy" developments for the 21st century. The SSPs are linked to the RCPs through the specification of a climate policy scenario. A specific SSP would lead to a certain radiative forcing level but, when combined with a specific climate policy scenario, the forcing levels would decrease to be in line with a lower RCP. Not all SSPs can be linked to all RCPs, either because the SSP without policy leads to lower forcing levels than described in the RCP (e.g. if an SSP without mitigation action leads to a radiative forcing level of 7 W/m[2] , it is incompatible with RCP8.5), or because the required stringency of climate policy involved makes it infeasible to reach very low forcing levels (i.e. if the required mitigation efforts are insufficient to reduce radiative forcing to the desired level).

evidence.section: A brief introduction to SSP scenarios
evidence.original_text: The different SSP storylines are described in O’Neill et al. (2014). These storylines are constructed around two axes: challenges to mitigation and challenges to adaptation, as illustrated in Fig. 1. A natural way of interpreting these axes could be to see emissions in absence of policies as index of the challenges to mitigation, and vulnerability to climate change as indicator of adaptation challenges. In SSP1, the challenges for both adaptation and mitigation to climate change are low, as relatively rapid income growth is combined with substantially reduced reliance on natural resources. This is achieved at least in part through quick technological change and through high levels of international cooperation. Also, following KC et al. (2010) and Lutz and KC (2012), high levels of education induce lower fertility rates and therefore smaller populations. Consequently, global emission levels are relatively low compared to most of the other scenarios.

A natural way of interpreting these axes could be to see emissions in absence of policies as index of the challenges to mitigation, and vulnerability to climate change as indicator of adaptation challenges. In SSP1, the challenges for both adaptation and mitigation to climate change are low, as relatively rapid income growth is combined with substantially reduced reliance on natural resources. This is achieved at least in part through quick technological change and through high levels of international cooperation. Also, following KC et al. (2010) and Lutz and KC (2012), high levels of education induce lower fertility rates and therefore smaller populations. Consequently, global emission levels are relatively low compared to most of the other scenarios.

evidence.section: Income inequality across countries in the SSPs
evidence.original_text: One ofthe major elementsin the SSPsisthe degree towhich there is a convergence in income across countries, or reversely, to what extent inequality between countries persists. The ENV-Growth model is based on the assumption that technological developments will at least partially spill over from the more developed to less developed economies. This implies that countries that currently have relatively low productivity levels will – ceteris paribus – have a higher economic growth rate than more developed countries. Other factors complicate this trend: for instance ageing populations will affect labour supply and thus limit growth in some countries more than others. More importantly, the SSPs have different assumptions on education levels, which imply that labour productivity is not identical across the SSPs. Thus, complex patterns of interactions affect growth rates of countries, and the resulting patterns of income convergence are not a priori clear. This section investigates income inequality in the income projections for the different SSPs. Only countries for which data is available from 1980 onwards are included (126 countries). It is important to remember that this analysis is carried out using constant PPP exchange rates.

This paper presents and compares a range of reference Gross Domestic Product (GDP) projections based on different perspectives on future socio-economic developments. The scenarios are based on the Shared Socioeconomic Pathways (SSPs). The SSP storylines have been developed by the climate change research community (Van Vuuren et al., 2014; O’Neill et al., 2014). They are part of a framework that combines the socio-economic developments defined by the SSPs with Representative greenhouse gas Concentration Pathways (RCPs) to assess future climatic changes (Moss et al., 2010; Van Vuuren et al., 2012). The economic growth projections developed here therefore represent quantitative data linked to each SSP that can be combined with other information to develop Reference (Baseline) Scenarios for climate impact assessment. These Reference Scenarios assume no climate policies; climate policy scenarios can be added to link the SSPs to the RCPs.

Specific attention is paid to the development of income generated from the exploitation of the natural resources crude oil and natural gas.

evidence.section: Income inequality across countries in the SSPs
evidence.original_text: One ofthe major elementsin the SSPsisthe degree towhich there is a convergence in income across countries, or reversely, to what extent inequality between countries persists. The ENV-Growth model is based on the assumption that technological developments will at least partially spill over from the more developed to less developed economies. This implies that countries that currently have relatively low productivity levels will – ceteris paribus – have a higher economic growth rate than more developed countries. This section investigates income inequality in the income projections for the different SSPs. Only countries for which data is available from 1980 onwards are included (126 countries). It is important to remember that this analysis is carried out using constant PPP exchange rates.

The ENV-Growth model is largely based on the "conditional convergence growth" methodology elaborated by the OECD Economics Department by Duval and de la Maisonneuve (2010) and recently updated to project real GDP for OECD countries as well as major emerging economies to 2060 (Johansson et al., 2013). Some differences in the methodologies between ENV-Growth and Johansson et al. (2013) should be underlined. Firstly, the ENV-Growth framework only deals with potential (i.e. full capacity) real GDP projections, and not with short- and medium-term output gaps stemming from excess capacity. Secondly the two exercises of projections adopt different assumptions about convergence of future capital to output ratios, where ENV-Growth assumes a slow convergence of capital to output ratios to a common long-term level. Thirdly, the ENVGrowth model has also been enhanced to include energy both as productive input (Foure´ et al., 2012) and as a generator of resource rents for oil and gas producing countries (World Bank, 2012a). In conclusion, these and other adaptations to the global scope and longer time horizon make the ENV-Growth projections incomparable to those by Johansson et al. (2013).

This section presents the main results from the SSP projections, analysing key indicators and growth drivers at global level and for selected regions. These projections provide a basis for quantitative analysis of environmental impacts associated with economic activity, but by themselves ignore the feedbacks from such environmental impacts to the economy. The underlying storylines are, however, constructed such that assumptions for each SSP are internally consistent, e.g. on availability of fossil fuel resources as input to economic activity. GDP and income levels are presented in 2005 USD using constant PPP. Over the century, it is likely that PPP exchange rates would also gradually converge, as productivity gains affect the structure of domestic economies. As illustrated in Fig. 3, global GDP levels by the end of the century vary substantially across SSPs. The range of global GDP levels at the end of the century varies from around 280 trillion USD in SSP3 to more than 1000 trillion USD in SSP5. This pattern is similar for income (i.e. per capita GDP) levels. SSP5, with its narrative focused on "conventional" economic development, projects a global GDP increase by 2100 of more than 15-fold the 2010 level. In this scenario growth rates of income remain above 2% per annum throughout the century, leading to a 14-fold increase of income by 2100. SSPs 3 and 4, which represent the scenarios with lowest levels of international co-operation and trade, are at the bottom of the range. They both see marked reductions in global growth of income to 0.5% and 0.7% per annum, respectively. The drop in global growth starts almost immediately in SSP3 while it is more gradual in SSP4, which first follows the growth pattern of the SSP2 scenario. SSP3 in particular shows very low growth in income (a bit more than doubling in income levels over the century), following the assumptions of low growth rates for the economic drivers. SSPs 1 and 2 have intermediate growth rates. In the first decades, SSP1 presents higher growth at global level as it assumes a quicker convergence. Given the higher population projections in SSP2, income levels diverge more than absolute GDP levels between SSP1 and SSP2. The transition from the short-term forecasts, as based on IMF and OECD projections, towards the long-term growth potentials, projected by the conditional convergence model, is visible in the growth rates. While there is some short-term differentiation between the SSPs, the different characteristics of the storylines only have a pronounced effect in the longer run. This reflects the short-term inertia in the economic system. The ranking for countries varies between the different SSPs. Fig. 4 illustrates results for a few selected countries: USA (an example of a technologically advanced, high-income economy), China (an example of a middle-income emerging economy), India (an example of an emerging economy that currently still has relatively low income) and Tanzania (an example of a low-income developing country). Tanzania was chosen because it provides a typical example of an African economy that is gradually moving out of poverty, and there have been no major shocks to the economy in recent years that affect the projections in atypical ways. Income levels (panel A) are highest in SSP5 for all the countries considered. However, there are substantial differences for the other SSPs. In particular, income in SSP4 is lower than in SSP2 in countries at lower stages of development, such as India and Tanzania, because the development barriers inherent in the SSP4 narrative prevent these countries from converging rapidly. The two SSPs are fairly similar in the case of China, while for the USA SSP4 leads to higher income levels. The figure also illustrates that income convergence is a slow process, as by 2050, Tanzania and India still have substantially lower income levels than China and especially the USA. By 2100, per capita GDP differences are much smaller in SSP1 and especially SSP5; the inequalities remain much sharper in SSP3 and SSP4.

entries.name: SSP GDP and income projections dataset

evidence.original_text: The first step of the calibration process consists in compiling a historical database for the 184 countries considered for the period 1960–2010. The OECD Economic Outlook database (June 2012 release; OECD, 2012b) is used for OECD countries, while the data for non-OECD countries draws upon the World Bank World Development Indicators (WDI) database (December 2012 release; World Bank, 2012a). All variables in real value terms (GDP, government expenditures) are converted to 2005 USD in Purchasing Power Parity (PPP) using the World Bank International Comparison Program (ICP) exchange rates. Data and historical trends are then extrapolated using short and medium run projections, when available, from either the OECD (2012b) or the IMF (World Economic Outlook database – September 2012; IMF, 2012). For countries covered by the OECD economic outlook database projections to 2013 are first applied, and then extended using IMF projections to 2017. For others countries IMF projections are used for the whole 2010–2017 period. If IMF projections are not available, the long-term model is directly used. The model projections effectively start in 2018. The calibration of historical and short term data is important because it influences projections by changing capital accumulation, the longterm frontier and the distance to it (see Section 7 and the Annex for more details). The labour force database (participation rates and employment rates by cohort and gender) is extracted from ILO (2011) active population prospects (up to 2020) and OECD Labour Force Statistics and Projections (2011). The long-term structural unemployment level is assumed to be 2% for all countries. Population and education data were taken from the contribution by Lutz and KC (2012) to the SSP framework (see Section 4.2). Historical energy demands were extracted from IEA Extended Energy Balance (2011) while the projections of energy efficiency improvements up to 2017 rely on IEA World Energy Outlook (2012), and then follow a rule of convergence towards leader economies in terms of energy efficiency. Natural resource rents in the base year 2010 are calculated by comparing world prices and domestic extraction costs, similar to how rents are compiled in World Bank (2012a), using oil and gas production costs from IEA World Energy Outlook (2011). Oil and gas reserves for 2010 are taken from BP (2011). The estimates for conventional resources are extracted from BGR (2012). Unconventional oil resources estimates (including Canada tar sands, Venezuela extra heavy oil and shale oil) are extracted from WEC (2007) while shale gas resources estimates are based on EIA (2011a). Physical capital stock was built-up from historical investment data series, assuming a 5% annual depreciation rate. As they are not directly observable, the historical total factor productivity and autonomous energy efficiency levels were derived by inverting the law of motion for the GDP and energy demand equations, respectively. Finally, a number of ad hoc assumptions were necessary to complete information, for countries with missing data. Specifically, in incidental cases where specific historical data was missing, data from various sources (e.g. domestic national datasets or CIA fact book) were used and growth rates from similar countries were applied.

entries.name: Historical and short-term economic database for SSP calibration

evidence.original_text: The OECD SSPs projections are based on the ENV-Growth modelling framework, which is used to project future levels of global and country-specific GDP and income, quantified as the average level of GDP per capita in a country (Chateau et al., 2014a). It is based on the assumption that each country gradually catches up to its own frontier level of income that is consistent with its endowments and institutions (Barro and Sala-i-Martin, 2004). This does not necessarily implythat absolute incomelevels of developing countries will gradually converge towards the levels of most developed economies, as local circumstances matter. GDP projections are generated using an augmented Solow growth model that includes accumulation of human capital (Mankiw et al., 1992). There is no unified theoretical model of economic growth (Blanchard and Fischer, 1996), and many ways of representing economic growth, and especially technological progress, exist. Macroeconometric models have been popular, but lack a theoretical foundation. They are subject to the Lucas Critique (Lucas, 1976), which states that models that are purely based on historical patterns cannot be used for policy advice, as they lack an explanation of the underlying structural parameters (including preferences and technologies). The augmented Solow growth model, which includes TFP as a main driver of growth, is sufficient to provide GDP per capita projections that are consistent with observed empirical data on economic growth – the so-called "new stylised facts of growth" (Mankiw et al., 1992; Easterly and Levine, 2001) – while keeping a very simple sectoral structure. Expanding the number of sectors would allow for a richer description of economic activity, but would also require additional data, which is not always available. That is why most large-scale multi-sectoral models calibrate first to macroeconomic projections and only specify sectoral economic activity in a second step (e.g. Chateau et al., 2014b). More advanced models have emerged in the literature that can have an endogenous representation of TFP growth (Barro and Sala-i-Martin, III, 2004), but endogenising TFP growth is much more relevant for policy analysis than for scenario analysis as performed here. A clear limitation of the augmented Solow model is that it assumes that convergence starts immediately, while in practice the timing is uncertain and there are barriers to convergence in some countries. This limitation is dealt with by modelling the near future on the economic forecasts by IMF and OECD, and adopting a gradual transition towards the long-term growth framework. The second limitation is that aggregate growth models are generally adapted to the characterisation of developed economies and less suited for developing economies. This is partially addressed by adding human capital as a driver of growth, and by integrating country-specific elements in the projections for TFP. A further extension of the model could be to explicitly include natural resources and land into the model; this is left for future research but can build on the general framework adopted here for fuel resources.

entries.description: Standardized GDP projection methodology using conditional convergence augmented Solow growth model

evidence.original_text: GDP and income levels are presented in 2005 USD using constant PPP. Over the century, it is likely that PPP exchange rates would also gradually converge, as productivity gains affect the structure of domestic economies. As illustrated in Fig. 3, global GDP levels by the end of the century vary substantially across SSPs. The range of global GDP levels at the end of the century varies from around 280 trillion USD in SSP3 to more than 1000 trillion USD in SSP5. This pattern is similar for income (i.e. per capita GDP) levels.

entries.description: GDP and income data expressed in constant 2005 USD PPP

evidence.section: 4.2. Interpretation of the economic dimension of the SSP storylines
evidence.original_text: The GDP projections for the various SSPs can be differentiated by the factors influencing growth, including exogenous demographic trends, education levels, the speed of convergence of income of less developed countries, technological progress, trade openness and the development of fossil fuel markets. The detailed assumptions on these factors for each SSP scenario are provided in Table 1. The assumptions in Table 1 aim to reflect the challenges in climate change adaptation and mitigation as outlined in the SSP storylines. For example, in SSP1 low population growth and high education levels reflect a world in which there are lower challenges for adaptation, while high population growth and low education levels reflect a world with high challenges for adaptation. Similarly, a high technological development in SSP1 reflects a more sustainable world with low challenges for climate change mitigation. The exploitation of the fossil fuel resources oil and gas is higher in a scenario with high growth as SSP5, reflecting high challenges for climate mitigation that follow from the intensive use of natural resources. Assumptions on technological development and fossil fuel resources have also been adapted to reflect the SPPs storylines. Drivers related to technological development are generally assumed to be greater in scenarios with higher international collaboration and more attention to sustainability. The rate of change of the technology frontier is a key driver of growth in high income countries, as these are closer to their frontier and depend less on convergence. This rate is set highest in SSP5 (which focuses on "conventional" economic development), followed by SSP1 (focused on "sustainable" growth), and set at a low value for SSP3 (where technological progress is much less rapid). Similarly, the speed of convergence and trade openness are (very) high in the SSP5, and low in the fragmented scenario SSP3. Assumptions related to TFP drivers in SSP4 (focused on "unequal" economic development paths) are differentiated between income groups, namely Low-Income (LI) countries, Middle-Income (MI) countries, and High-Income (HI) countries, to reflect the regional inequalities and high barriers to international cooperation.

entries.description: SSP-specific assumptions for key growth drivers including population, education, TFP, convergence speed, trade openness, and fossil fuel resources

evidence.original_text: The OECD SSPs projections are based on the ENV-Growth modelling framework, which is used to project future levels of global and country-specific GDP and income, quantified as the average level of GDP per capita in a country (Chateau et al., 2014a). It is based on the assumption that each country gradually catches up to its own frontier level of income that is consistent with its endowments and institutions (Barro and Sala-i-Martin, 2004). This does not necessarily imply that absolute income levels of developing countries will gradually converge towards the levels of most developed economies, as local circumstances matter. GDP projections are generated using an augmented Solow growth model that includes accumulation of human capital (Mankiw et al., 1992). There is no unified theoretical model of economic growth (Blanchard and Fischer, 1996), and many ways of representing economic growth, and especially technological progress, exist. Macroeconometric models have been popular, but lack a theoretical foundation. They are subject to the Lucas Critique (Lucas, 1976), which states that models that are purely based on historical patterns cannot be used for policy advice, as they lack an explanation of the underlying structural parameters (including preferences and technologies). The augmented Solow growth model, which includes TFP as a main driver of growth, is sufficient to provide GDP per capita projections that are consistent with observed empirical data on economic growth – the so-called "new stylised facts of growth" (Mankiw et al., 1992; Easterly and Levine, 2001) – while keeping a very simple sectoral structure. Expanding the number of sectors would allow for a richer description of economic activity, but would also require additional data, which is not always available. That is why most large-scale multi-sectoral models calibrate first to macroeconomic projections and only specify sectoral economic activity in a second step (e.g. Chateau et al., 2014b). More advanced models have emerged in the literature that can have an endogenous representation of TFP growth (Barro and Sala-i-Martin, 2004), but endogenising TFP growth is much more relevant for policy analysis than for scenario analysis as performed here.

The ENV-Growth model is largely based on the "conditional convergence growth" methodology elaborated by the OECD Economics Department by Duval and de la Maisonneuve (2010) and recently updated to project real GDP for OECD countries as well as major emerging economies to 2060 (Johansson et al., 2013). The ENV-Growth model, described in the Annex, applies most elements of this methodology to a longer timeframe, until the end of the century, and to a larger set of countries, including most nonOECD countries. Some differences in the methodologies between ENV-Growth and Johansson et al. (2013) should be underlined. Firstly, the ENV-Growth framework only deals with potential (i.e. full capacity) real GDP projections, and not with short- and medium-term output gaps stemming from excess capacity. Secondly the two exercises of projections adopt different assumptions about convergence of future capital to output ratios, where ENV-Growth assumes a slow convergence of capital to output ratios to a common long-term level. Thirdly, the ENVGrowth model has also been enhanced to include energy both as productive input (Foure´ et al., 2012) and as a generator of resource rents for oil and gas producing countries (World Bank, 2012a). In conclusion, these and other adaptations to the global scope and longer time horizon make the ENV-Growth projections incomparable to those by Johansson et al. (2013).

evidence.section: Annex. Methodology of the ENV-Growth model
evidence.original_text: This growth framework is based on a Solow (1956), while featuring as additional inputs human capital (as in Mankiw et al., 1992) and energy (as in Foure´ et al., 2012). GDP (Y) is calculated as a function of capital (K), efficient labour (a combination of human capital h and the labour force potential employment L), TFP (A), energy (E), autonomous energy efficiency (l[E] ) plus the value added of the natural resource exploitation sector that includes oil and gas extraction (VA[NR] ): Y = A * (K^α) * ((h * L)^β) * (E^γ) * (l[E]^δ) + VA[NR]. In the model the continuous improvement in TFP (A) leads to more efficient production as more output can be created with the same combination of primary factors. TFP depends on the existing TFP levels and on the long-term TFP frontier (A[LT] ): A_t = A_t-1 * (1 + r * (A[LT]_t-1 / A_t-1 - 1)). TFP growth is assumed to be a combination of two elements: (i) countries gradually converge towards their "long-term TFP frontier" (driven by the speed of convergence); (ii) the long-term TFP frontier shifts over time. As the long term TFP frontier is country-specific, all countries will grow through both channels (which are often termed "technological catch up" and "technological pass-through", respectively). More technologically advanced countries, however, are closer to their frontier and therefore, ceteris paribus, grow less rapidly than countries which are less technologically advanced (i.e. whose distance to their long-term TFP frontier is longer).

The first step of the calibration process consists in compiling a historical database for the 184 countries considered for the period 1960–2010. The OECD Economic Outlook database (June 2012 release; OECD, 2012b) is used for OECD countries, while the data for non-OECD countries draws upon the World Bank World Development Indicators (WDI) database (December 2012 release; World Bank, 2012a). All variables in real value terms (GDP, government expenditures) are converted to 2005 USD in Purchasing Power Parity (PPP) using the World Bank International Comparison Program (ICP) exchange rates. Data and historical trends are then extrapolated using short and medium run projections, when available, from either the OECD (2012b) or the IMF (World Economic Outlook database – September 2012; IMF, 2012). For countries covered by the OECD economic outlook database projections to 2013 are first applied, and then extended using IMF projections to 2017. For others countries IMF projections are used for the whole 2010–2017 period. If IMF projections are not available, the long-term model is directly used. The model projections effectively start in 2018. The calibration of historical and short term data is important because it influences projections by changing capital accumulation, the longterm frontier and the distance to it (see Section 7 and the Annex for more details). The labour force database (participation rates and employment rates by cohort and gender) is extracted from ILO (2011) active population prospects (up to 2020) and OECD Labour Force Statistics and Projections (2011). The long-term structural unemployment level is assumed to be 2% for all countries. Population and education data were taken from the contribution by Lutz and KC (2012) to the SSP framework (see Section 4.2). Historical energy demands were extracted from IEA Extended Energy Balance (2011) while the projections of energy efficiency improvements up to 2017 rely on IEA World Energy Outlook (2012), and then follow a rule of convergence towards leader economies in terms of energy efficiency. Natural resource rents in the base year 2010 are calculated by comparing world prices and domestic extraction costs, similar to how rents are compiled in World Bank (2012a), using oil and gas production costs from IEA World Energy Outlook (2011). Oil and gas reserves for 2010 are taken from BP (2011). The estimates for conventional resources are extracted from BGR (2012). Unconventional oil resources estimates (including Canada tar sands, Venezuela extra heavy oil and shale oil) are extracted from WEC (2007) while shale gas resources estimates are based on EIA (2011a). Physical capital stock was built-up from historical investment data series, assuming a 5% annual depreciation rate. As they are not directly observable, the historical total factor productivity and autonomous energy efficiency levels were derived by inverting the law of motion for the GDP and energy demand equations, respectively. Finally, a number of ad hoc assumptions were necessary to complete information, for countries with missing data. Specifically, in incidental cases where specific historical data was missing, data from various sources (e.g. domestic national datasets or CIA fact book) were used and growth rates from similar countries were applied.

entries.setup: historical database compilation for 184 countries (1960-2010), using OECD Economic Outlook, World Bank WDI, IMF WEO, ILO, IEA, BP, BGR, WEC, EIA datasets

evidence.section: 4.2. Interpretation of the economic dimension of the SSP storylines
evidence.original_text: The GDP projections for the various SSPs can be differentiated by the factors influencing growth, including exogenous demographic trends, education levels, the speed of convergence of income of less developed countries, technological progress, trade openness and the development of fossil fuel markets. The detailed assumptions on these factors for each SSP scenario are provided in Table 1. The assumptions in Table 1 aim to reflect the challenges in climate change adaptation and mitigation as outlined in the SSP storylines. For example, in SSP1 low population growth and high education levels reflect a world in which there are lower challenges for adaptation, while high population growth and low education levels reflect a world with high challenges for adaptation. Similarly, a high technological development in SSP1 reflects a more sustainable world with low challenges for climate change mitigation. The exploitation of the fossil fuel resources oil and gas is higher in a scenario with high growth as SSP5, reflecting high challenges for climate mitigation that follow from the intensive use of natural resources. Assumptions on technological development and fossil fuel resources have also been adapted to reflect the SPPs storylines. Drivers related to technological development are generally assumed to be greater in scenarios with higher international collaboration and more attention to sustainability. The rate of change of the technology frontier is a key driver of growth in high income countries, as these are closer to their frontier and depend less on convergence. This rate is set highest in SSP5 (which focuses on "conventional" economic development), followed by SSP1 (focused on "sustainable" growth), and set at a low value for SSP3 (where technological progress is much less rapid). Similarly, the speed of convergence and trade openness are (very) high in the SSP5, and low in the fragmented scenario SSP3. Assumptions related to TFP drivers in SSP4 (focused on "unequal" economic development paths) are differentiated between income groups, namely Low-Income (LI) countries, Middle-Income (MI) countries, and High-Income (HI) countries, to reflect the regional inequalities and high barriers to international cooperation.

entries.task: SSP scenario-specific parameterization of ENV-Growth model
entries.setup: quantification of SSP-specific assumptions for TFP-related growth drivers (speed of convergence, trade openness, product market regulations, fossil fuel resource exploitation)

evidence.section: 7. The influence of changes in short-term growth rates on longterm income levels
evidence.original_text: Changes in the forecasts of short-term income growth rates affect long-term income levels. This is illustrated by comparing long-term projections based on the 2013 short-term forecasts (base model) with earlier short-term forecasts from 2011. Given the large uncertainties in recent short-term forecasts, it is necessary to conduct such a sensitivity analysis to assess the impact of the recent economic crisis and uncertainty in short term developments on longer term growth trends. The interaction between the short and long run economic developments entails a direct level effect from the difference in short-term income levels, as well as an array of indirect effects through capital accumulation, changes in the distance from the long-term technology frontier (and hence convergence speed), changes in the long-term frontier itself, and energy resource scarcity. Fig. 11 illustrates the change in short-term forecasts and the influence on long term income levels for a 32-regional aggregation of the individual country results. A positive change in 2016 means that the base model, using the most recent estimates, is more pessimistic on short-term growth than the older forecasts (and thus changing to the older forecasts boosts short-term income levels). The assumption is made that the differences in short-term forecasts reflect a productivity shock, which affects the distance from the long-term technology frontier and hence the convergence speed. Changes in the long-term frontier itself, adjustment of capital accumulation and energy resource scarcity also play a role, although minor, in this adjustment. As illustrated in Fig. 12, the fundamental drivers of long-term growth are not – or hardly – affected by the short-term shock, and income levels gradually return to the levels of the base model. After the imposed shock in 2016, there is an immediate adjustment possibility in 2017 through changes in investment levels, but after that the model shows essentially a gradual phase-out of the shock. Alternatively, if the change in short-term forecasts were interpreted purely as a business-cycle shock and implemented as a change in employment levels, the adjustment process would be virtually instantaneous, as the model does not capture inertia in the labour market.

entries.task: sensitivity analysis of short-term growth forecasts on long-term income levels
entries.setup: comparison of long-term projections based on 2013 short-term forecasts (base model) with earlier short-term forecasts from 2011 for SSP2

evidence.original_text: As illustrated in Fig. 3, global GDP levels by the end of the century vary substantially across SSPs. The range of global GDP levels at the end of the century varies from around 280 trillion USD in SSP3 to more than 1000 trillion USD in SSP5. This pattern is similar for income (i.e. per capita GDP) levels. SSP5, with its narrative focused on "conventional" economic development, projects a global GDP increase by 2100 of more than 15-fold the 2010 level. In this scenario growth rates of income remain above 2% per annum throughout the century, leading to a 14-fold increase of income by 2100. SSPs 3 and 4, which represent the scenarios with lowest levels of international co-operation and trade, are at the bottom of the range. They both see marked reductions in global growth of income to 0.5% and 0.7% per annum, respectively. The drop in global growth starts almost immediately in SSP3 while it is more gradual in SSP4, which first follows the growth pattern of the SSP2 scenario. SSP3 in particular shows very low growth in income (a bit more than doubling in income levels over the century), following the assumptions of low growth rates for the economic drivers. SSPs 1 and 2 have intermediate growth rates. In the first decades, SSP1 presents higher growth at global level as it assumes a quicker convergence. Given the higher population projections in SSP2, income levels diverge more than absolute GDP levels between SSP1 and SSP2. The transition from the short-term forecasts, as based on IMF and OECD projections, towards the long-term growth potentials, projected by the conditional convergence model, is visible in the growth rates. While there is some short-term differentiation between the SSPs, the different characteristics of the storylines only have a pronounced effect in the longer run. This reflects the short-term inertia in the economic system.

entries.context: SSP3 scenario by the end of the century (2100), expressed in 2005 USD using constant PPP

evidence.original_text: As illustrated in Fig. 3, global GDP levels by the end of the century vary substantially across SSPs. The range of global GDP levels at the end of the century varies from around 280 trillion USD in SSP3 to more than 1000 trillion USD in SSP5. This pattern is similar for income (i.e. per capita GDP) levels. SSP5, with its narrative focused on "conventional" economic development, projects a global GDP increase by 2100 of more than 15-fold the 2010 level. In this scenario growth rates of income remain above 2% per annum throughout the century, leading to a 14-fold increase of income by 2100. SSPs 3 and 4, which represent the scenarios with lowest levels of international co-operation and trade, are at the bottom of the range. They both see marked reductions in global growth of income to 0.5% and 0.7% per annum, respectively. The drop in global growth starts almost immediately in SSP3 while it is more gradual in SSP4, which first follows the growth pattern of the SSP2 scenario. SSP3 in particular shows very low growth in income (a bit more than doubling in income levels over the century), following the assumptions of low growth rates for the economic drivers. SSPs 1 and 2 have intermediate growth rates. In the first decades, SSP1 presents higher growth at global level as it assumes a quicker convergence. Given the higher population projections in SSP2, income levels diverge more than absolute GDP levels between SSP1 and SSP2. The transition from the short-term forecasts, as based on IMF and OECD projections, towards the long-term growth potentials, projected by the conditional convergence model, is visible in the growth rates. While there is some short-term differentiation between the SSPs, the different characteristics of the storylines only have a pronounced effect in the longer run. This reflects the short-term inertia in the economic system.

entries.context: SSP5 scenario by the end of the century (2100), expressed in 2005 USD using constant PPP

evidence.original_text: Projections of global and regional income levels (in 2005 USD) differ widely across the different SSPs. Globally, the range of average per capita income levels at the end of the century varies from just over 25 thousand USD (a less than 3-fold increase over current levels) in SSP3 to more than 150 thousand USD in SSP5 (a 16-fold increase). In 2050 the range is smaller, but still substantial, at 16–41 thousand USD. In SSPs 1, 2 and 5 there is substantial income convergence, but global income inequalities are much more persistent in SSPs 3 and 4, with income in the poorest countries limited to around 10 thousand USD. Such a variation of income levels across scenarios, illustrates the difference in challenges that the five SSPs imply for climate change mitigation and adaptation: the relatively low income levels in vulnerable developing countries in SSP3 and SSP4 suggest high challenges to adaptation, whereas the high level of energy-intensive economic activity in SSP3 and SSP5 indicate high mitigation challenges. Nonetheless, the link between economic activity and these challenges is complex, and more detailed analysis is needed to identify the adaptation and mitigation challenges that result from the income projections presented here.

entries.context: SSP3 scenario by the end of the century (2100), expressed in 2005 USD

evidence.original_text: Projections of global and regional income levels (in 2005 USD) differ widely across the different SSPs. Globally, the range of average per capita income levels at the end of the century varies from just over 25 thousand USD (a less than 3-fold increase over current levels) in SSP3 to more than 150 thousand USD in SSP5 (a 16-fold increase). In 2050 the range is smaller, but still substantial, at 16–41 thousand USD. In SSPs 1, 2 and 5 there is substantial income convergence, but global income inequalities are much more persistent in SSPs 3 and 4, with income in the poorest countries limited to around 10 thousand USD. Such a variation of income levels across scenarios, illustrates the difference in challenges that the five SSPs imply for climate change mitigation and adaptation: the relatively low income levels in vulnerable developing countries in SSP3 and SSP4 suggest high challenges to adaptation, whereas the high level of energy-intensive economic activity in SSP3 and SSP5 indicate high mitigation challenges. Nonetheless, the link between economic activity and these challenges is complex, and more detailed analysis is needed to identify the adaptation and mitigation challenges that result from the income projections presented here.

entries.context: SSP5 scenario by the end of the century (2100), expressed in 2005 USD

evidence.original_text: As illustrated in Fig. 3, global GDP levels by the end of the century vary substantially across SSPs. The range of global GDP levels at the end of the century varies from around 280 trillion USD in SSP3 to more than 1000 trillion USD in SSP5. SSP5, with its narrative focused on "conventional" economic development, projects a global GDP increase by 2100 of more than 15-fold the 2010 level. SSPs 3 and 4, which represent the scenarios with lowest levels of international co-operation and trade, are at the bottom of the range. SSPs 1 and 2 have intermediate growth rates. In the first decades, SSP1 presents higher growth at global level as it assumes a quicker convergence. Given the higher population projections in SSP2, income levels diverge more than absolute GDP levels between SSP1 and SSP2. The ranking for countries varies between the different SSPs. Fig. 4 illustrates results for a few selected countries: USA (an example of a technologically advanced, high-income economy), China (an example of a middle-income emerging economy), India (an example of an emerging economy that currently still has relatively low income) and Tanzania (an example of a low-income developing country). Income levels (panel A) are highest in SSP5 for all the countries considered. However, there are substantial differences for the other SSPs. In particular, income in SSP4 is lower than in SSP2 in countries at lower stages of development, such as India and Tanzania, because the development barriers inherent in the SSP4 narrative prevent these countries from converging rapidly. The two SSPs are fairly similar in the case of China, while for the USA SSP4 leads to higher income levels.

evidence.section: 6. Income inequality across countries in the SSPs
evidence.original_text: As shown in Fig. 8, global income inequality (currently equal to 0.66 for the sample of countries considered and for the USD2005PPP exchange rates, down from 0.85 in 1980) reduces swiftly in SSPs 1, 2 and 5, reaching the values of 0.13, 0.23 and 0.11, respectively, by the end of the century. In SSPs 3 and 4, international income inequality differences are much more persistent. This is not surprising given that these two scenarios are based on storylines reflecting a persistent inequality and a lower economic convergence. For SSP3, the Gini coefficient is rather stable over time (after a small drop in the short run determined by the short-term forecasts that is common to all scenarios), while all countries are projected to grow slowly, there is some convergence, but the dispersion remains substantial. In the first decades of SSP4 pathway, when the emerging economies largely catch up with the advanced economies, the Gini coefficient continues to fall. But a large group of least developed countries remains behind, and is projected to observe a much slower convergence process. This implies that over time, the Gini coefficient starts growing again and eventually almost gets back to current levels.

This paper presents and compares a range of reference Gross Domestic Product (GDP) projections based on different perspectives on future socio-economic developments. The scenarios are based on the Shared Socioeconomic Pathways (SSPs). The SSP storylines have been developed by the climate change research community (Van Vuuren et al., 2014; O’Neill et al., 2014). They are part of a framework that combines the socio-economic developments defined by the SSPs with Representative greenhouse gas Concentration Pathways (RCPs) to assess future climatic changes (Moss et al., 2010; Van Vuuren et al., 2012). The economic growth projections developed here therefore represent quantitative data linked to each SSP that can be combined with other information to develop Reference (Baseline) Scenarios for climate impact assessment. These Reference Scenarios assume no climate policies; climate policy scenarios can be added to link the SSPs to the RCPs. This paper illustrates a detailed methodology for making consistent long-term economic projections for most countries in the world, using the ENV-Growth model (Chateau et al., 2014a). ENV-Growth projects a gradual process of convergence towards a balanced growth path along the lines of an augmented Solow growth model (Barro and Sala-i-Martin, 2004). Projections start in 2018 since the model initially mimics short-term (2012–2017) economic projections of the OECD and the International Monetary Fund (IMF), and then follows a so-called conditional convergence hypothesis: income levels (e.g. GDP per capita) of less developed countries will increase faster than those of more developed economies based on a catch-up effect in the key drivers of per capita economic growth. Specific attention is paid to the development of income generated from the exploitation of the natural resources crude oil and natural gas. The methodology is applied to construct pathways of GDP and income (GDP per capita) levels for 184 individual countries, collectively representing 99.9% of global GDP in 2010, plus a stylised "rest of the world" region. Trend projections are made for each of the SSP scenarios by translating SSP storylines into assumptions on the various drivers of growth. This set of scenarios provides a range of future projections of GDP and income for the rest of the 21st century. The SSP scenarios do not cover the full spectrum of plausible economic projections, but they do illustrate a substantial variance in global GDP levels by the end of the century. The methodology can therefore also serve as a basis for different quantitative assessments that involve economic baselines.

This paper presented a methodology, adapted from recent work by OECD’s Economics Department (Johansson et al., 2013), for making stylised, consistent long-term economic projections for most countries in the world. The ENV-Growth model, based on a gradual process of conditional convergence towards a balanced growth path, was introduced and used to project different scenarios to be used as a reference for future projections of the impact of international climate change (and other) policies. The methodology goes beyond the usual drivers of economic growth in Solow growth models (total factor productivity, labour, physical capital and human capital) to explicitly account for efficiency improvements in energy use and the exploitation of fossil fuel resources (oil and gas). The model is calibrated for 184 countries, and provides projections for the entire century. The model has been applied to construct illustrative pathways of GDP and income (GDP per capita) levels for each of the five SSP scenarios. Projections of global and regional income levels (in 2005 USD) differ widely across the different SSPs. Globally, the range of average per capita income levels at the end of the century varies from just over 25 thousand USD (a less than 3-fold increase over current levels) in SSP3 to more than 150 thousand USD in SSP5 (a 16-fold increase). In 2050 the range is smaller, but still substantial, at 16–41 thousand USD. In SSPs 1, 2 and 5 there is substantial income convergence, but global income inequalities are much more persistent in SSPs 3 and 4, with income in the poorest countries limited to around 10 thousand USD. Such a variation of income levels across scenarios, illustrates the difference in challenges that the five SSPs imply for climate change mitigation and adaptation: the relatively low income levels in vulnerable developing countries in SSP3 and SSP4 suggest high challenges to adaptation, whereas the high level of energy-intensive economic activity in SSP3 and SSP5 indicate high mitigation challenges. Nonetheless, the link between economic activity and these challenges is complex, and more detailed analysis is needed to identify the adaptation and mitigation challenges that result from the income projections presented here. Energy efficiency improvements, while vital to avoid major resource constraints, have in general only a moderate effect on economic growth, when compared to (total factor) productivity, physical capital accumulation and demographic developments. For resource-rich countries, reduced income from depleting conventional resources will put a downward pressure on growth, although some countries can (partially) alleviate this by exploiting more non-conventional sources, especially in SSP3 and SSP5 (the scenarios with high fossil energy demand).

It should be noted that the projections presented in this paper are not predictions of what will happen. They are meant as reference projections for quantitative modelling exercises that rely on long-term global economic baselines. For instance, Integrated Assessment Models (IAMs) need GDP projections in order to derive long-term emission and temperature projections for at least a century ahead. Having a common set of economic growth assumptions across IAMs helps understand the differences in projections of emissions. Similarly, long-term projections for agricultural sectors can benefit from reference projections on economic activity, as these influence various areas e.g. food demand. The projections are subject to large uncertainties, particularly at the country level and for later decades, and should be interpreted with sufficient care. While an internally consistent set of assumptions is used to make these projections, they disregard a wide range of country-specific drivers of economic growth that are outside the narrow economic framework, such as external shocks, governance barriers and feedbacks from environmental damage. Further, these projections are not official OECD projections, but customised projections made by OECD specialists specifically for the SSP scenarios.

A clear limitation of the augmented Solow model is that it assumes that convergence starts immediately, while in practice the timing is uncertain and there are barriers to convergence in some countries. This limitation is dealt with by modelling the near future on the economic forecasts by IMF and OECD, and adopting a gradual transition towards the long-term growth framework. The second limitation is that aggregate growth models are generally adapted to the characterisation of developed economies and less suited for developing economies. This is partially addressed by adding human capital as a driver of growth, and by integrating country-specific elements in the projections for TFP. A further extension of the model could be to explicitly include natural resources and land into the model; this is left for future research but can build on the general framework adopted here for fuel resources.

One should, however, not forget the large uncertainties involved in projecting country income levels over an entire century. Furthermore, major external shocks, for instance in the form of natural disasters, discovery of valuable resources or military conflict, can occur abruptly and affect projections severely for prolonged periods of time, and in some cases even affect economic growth trends permanently. Governance barriers to growth are also not considered. Moreover, these projections ignore terms of trade effects, changes in PPPs and feedbacks from environment to the economy. The projections are not predictions, and should be interpreted with sufficient caution. Differences between SSPs are for example more robust than individual projections, and average growth rates are more robust than specific GDP and income levels.

A further extension of the model could be to explicitly include natural resources and land into the model; this is left for future research but can build on the general framework adopted here for fuel resources.

These caveats notwithstanding, the set of five reference socio-economic projections illustrated in this paper can be used in several ways. First, when making new baseline scenarios, modellers can choose one or more SSPs and use the quantitative data available, together with some qualitative information that is given in the SSP storylines. Second, SSPs could also be used to position baselines that are produced using domestic or alternative data sources. Projection elements in common with the SSP storylines (e.g. income or emissions) can be identified, thus mapping baselines to SSP projections. A combination of these two approaches is also possible, e.g. where domestic population projections are combined with income projections from the most closely related SSP. Finally, while this set of scenarios is constructed specifically for future climate change policy analysis, the resulting projections can also be used for a wider range of studies. The projections are suitable as a reference for quantitative analysis that relies on long-term economic baselines as they reflect different combinations of underlying growth drivers.
